original_abstract:
This paper examines the rapid expansion of innovative activity in India and China, as measured by U.S. patent grants, and challenges the conventional view that these emerging giants are already rivaling the technological leadership of advanced economies through purely indigenous means. By analyzing USPTO patent data and citation patterns, the authors demonstrate that the recent surge in patenting from these regions is primarily driven by multinational corporations (MNCs) and is characterized by a high degree of international coinvention.

The study provides evidence of a global vertical disintegration of R&D, where the personal and technical resources of emerging markets are integrated into the global innovation networks of Western firms. While Indian and Chinese inventors are contributing more to global technology, their output—especially in India—is often associated with international collaborations and remains, on average, less impactful than the research conducted by the same MNCs in their home countries. Regression analyses specifically show a significant "multisponsorship premium," where local patents owned by MNCs or produced through international teams command higher citation counts than those produced by purely indigenous firms. Furthermore, the authors utilize field interviews with R&D managers and an analysis of Chinese domestic patent data to argue that the quality of truly indigenous innovation still lags behind Western standards. The findings suggest that while India and China have become vital nodes in a globalized division of labor, their rise is currently complementary to, rather than a direct substitute for, the high-level innovation of advanced industrial economies.
humanized_abstract:
We examine the pace of emergence of India and China as centres of new innovation. Using USPTO patent data, we challenge the view that India and China are emerging centres of technological leadership in their own right, and examine the role of multinational corporations (MNCs) in the surge in patents being granted to these developing countries. Our methodology is quantitative and based on new patent data on India and China for the period 1992–2004 as well as citation-based analyses of their patent stocks. Our main findings show that recent increases in patents coming from India and China reflect the growing presence of MNCs in the two countries, with many patents being the result of collaborative work between MNCs and local scientists in India and China, and foreign scientists working in America, Europe and Japan.

In this study we show that the R&D activities of emerging markets are vertically dis-aggregated from the core of the Western technological system and horizontally integrated with it. Indian and Chinese inventors are doing more for global technology, but the work that they do is, at least in India, frequently part of international collaborations and is of lower average impact than the work done by the same MNCs at home. Using regression analysis, we find the existence of a multisponsorship premium: that is the patents owned by local firms or co-authored with multiple sponsors are more highly cited than those patents owned/authored by a single local firm. We back up this finding using field interviews with R&D managers for MNCs and using data on the impact of patents granted in China. Our conclusion is that emerging markets are performing key tasks in the global innovation system but the current contribution of India and China to this system is complementary to the contributions of the technology leaders of the West and not competitive. Indeed the current contribution of emerging markets to the global technological system is part of a division of labor in which India and China are merely a part of the global labour force.
